items.correct: A unit of DISTANCE - the distance light travels in one year.
items.why: Students hear 'year' and assume time. Light year is distance (about 9.46 trillion km) - it's a useful astronomical scale, not a measure of when something happened.

items.correct: Earth's axial tilt - 23.5 degrees - causes different hemispheres to receive more direct sunlight at different times of year.
items.misconception: Earth is closer to the Sun in summer.
items.random_wrong: The Sun's intensity changes throughout the year.
items.why: Students assume distance causes seasons, but Earth is closest to the Sun in January (Northern Hemisphere winter). The TILT changes the angle of sunlight and day length - that's what drives seasons.

items.q: Where is our Solar System located in the Milky Way?
items.correct: About two-thirds of the way out from the centre, in one of the spiral arms.
items.misconception: Right at the centre of the galaxy.
items.why: Students assume our Sun is central (Copernican habits die hard). We're in an unremarkable position about 26,000 light years from the centre, in the Orion Spur, a minor branch of the Milky Way.

items.q: Approximate length of a synodic month?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 29.5 days. “27.3 days” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Phase when the lit side faces away from Earth?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: New Moon. “Full Moon” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many days in a synodic month (rounded)?
items.why: Exact answer: 30 days. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
